evidence_quotes.quote: I would like to request this extend to the protection of video games that are being removed, permanently, from people after having purchased.
Games like "Warcraft 3" is unplayable in its original state due to a "reforged" version releasing that overwrites the original.
The Grand theft auto games (Vice city, 3 and San andreas) were removed to make room for "updated" versions that were universally hailed as poor immitations.
There is also the matter of games being shut down completely.
"The Crew" from Ubisoft comes to mind as a game that is completely unplayable now that servers have been shut down.
"Tony Hawk Pro Skater 5" s disc was essentially a download code and now the game is rendered unplayable.
"Anthem" has zero offline capabilities and in 2026 will be fully shutdown and unavailable to be played.
People have paid money for these games.
Having them inaccessible is not only unfair, but should be criminal.
One does not purchase a film disc with the expectation that eventually the rights holder will make a patch that will inactivate your film.
Why should this be okay for interactive media such as gaming.
Gaming has more immersive stories and attatchments than films and games are being killed off so the rightsholders can shove a "new" version to be purchased.
A fair act would be a plan for people to keep enjoying these games offline after support ends.
Just as a good deal of film being lost in its early days is seen as a tragedy, this doesn't need to be one.
This can prevent us from losing access to our games, our culture, our experiences, our purchses.

paragraphs.text: I would like to request this extend to the protection of video games that are being removed, permanently, from people after having purchased. Games like "Warcraft 3" is unplayable in its original state due to a "reforged" version releasing that overwrites the original. "Overwatch 1" was removed and replaced with "Overwatch 2". The Grand theft auto games (Vice city, 3 and San andreas) were removed to make room for "updated" versions that were universally hailed as poor immitations. There is also the matter of games being shut down completely. "The Crew" from Ubisoft comes to mind as a game that is completely unplayable now that servers have been shut down. "Tony Hawk Pro Skater 5" s disc was essentially a download code and now the game is rendered unplayable. "Anthem" has zero offline capabilities and in 2026 will be fully shutdown and unavailable to be played. People have paid money for these games. Having them inaccessible is not only unfair, but should be criminal. One does not purchase a film disc with the expectation that eventually the rights holder will make a patch that will inactivate your film. Why should this be okay for interactive media such as gaming. Gaming has more immersive stories and attatchments than films and games are being killed off so the rightsholders can shove a "new" version to be purchased. A fair act would be a plan for people to keep enjoying these games offline after support ends. Just as a good deal of film being lost in its early days is seen as a tragedy, this doesn't need to be one. This can prevent us from losing access to our games, our culture, our experiences, our purchses.
